title: Breaking Records in Quantum Computing
content:
Chinese scientists have achieved a monumental breakthrough in optical quantum information technology with the development of a programmable quantum computing prototype called Jiuzhang 4.0. According to a study published in the journal Nature, a research team led by the University of Science and Technology of China used the prototype to solve the complex Gaussian boson sampling problem. Remarkably, Jiuzhang 4.0 completed this task at a speed more than 10 to the 54th times faster than the world’s most powerful traditional supercomputer. The researchers successfully manipulated and detected quantum states of up to 3,050 photons. This represents a major technological leap from the 255 photons achieved with the previous iteration, Jiuzhang 3.0.

Currently, mainstream quantum computing technological routes include superconducting, ion trap, photonic, and neutral atom systems. The Jiuzhang series specifically encodes quantum bits using photons and performs quantum computation through the precise manipulation and measurement of these light particles. Since its initial successful construction in 2020, the prototype series has undergone several rapid upgrades. Each new version has further solidified the concept of quantum computational advantage while repeatedly setting new world records. This ongoing progress suggests that practical, large-scale quantum computing may arrive sooner than experts previously anticipated.
